title: Routing Absorption in Sparse Attention: Why Random Gates Are Hard to Beat
abstract: This paper investigates why end-to-end sparse attention training fails when the structure clearly exists. The authors introduce the concept of 'routing absorption', where the model's parameters continuously adapt to compensate for any mask imposed, making the gate's predictions carry little more information about attention structure than chance. The paper presents four controlled experiments on a 31M-parameter model to isolate different aspects of the absorption mechanism.
key_findings: In end-to-end training, the model's parameters adapt to compensate for the gate's mask
The gate's mask is 'absorbed' into the Q/K/V representations
Removing the gate or replacing it with random noise changes almost nothing
The gate's predictions carry little more information about attention structure than chance
experiments: Four controlled experiments on a 31M-parameter model
Preliminary scale evidence from Qwen3-1.7B (55× larger scale)
related_work: The paper connects its results to the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) literature
conclusions: The paper concludes that end-to-end sparse attention training fails due to routing absorption, where the model's parameters adapt to compensate for the gate's mask.
